subject: FREN
course_description: Through a variety of texts and artifacts, including but not restricted to literature, travelogues, legal documents, medical, historical, and political treatises, visual arts, students are introduced to specific interdisciplinary issues in French and Francophone Studies. Taught in English.
course_notes: (Same as HLTH 385 10) FREN 341: Illness Narratives / Professor Vincent Bruyère (Tuesdays/Thursdays 1:00-2:15PM. Content: This course explores illness narratives as a literary and cultural form through which individuals make sense of the body, suffering, and care. Approaching illness narratives as a genre, an archive of lived experience, and at times a form of self-care, the class examines how writing, film, and other cultural forms transform experiences of illness into meaning, testimony, and critique. Our perspective will be both historical and interdisciplinary. We will trace the emergence of narratives about illness from early modern anatomical writing to contemporary reflections on cancer and chronic illness, engaging works by authors and artists from Michel de Montaigne to Agnès Varda. Along the way, we will consider how illness narratives intersect with questions of gender, disability, vulnerability, and health activism. Through close reading, discussion, and critical analysis, students will reflect on how cultural representations of illness shape understandings of the body, care, and medical authority. The course also invites students to think about illness narratives as spaces of resistance, solidarity, and ethical reflection. The course is taught in English, with the option to complete written assignments in French.
